title: Describing Things Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Welcome! 👋 Unit 4. Colours, sizes, clothes — how to describe the world around you.
Five sections: a **clothes-shop dialogue**, **twelve colours**, **opposite adjectives**, **clothes vocabulary**, and the grammar behind why they all **change to match the noun**. Let's go!
The scene: you're in a clothes shop. You need a red shirt. Kick off with:
Excuse me, I'm looking for a red shirt. The shopkeeper holds one up:
Do you like this red shirt? Notice how 'red' takes its feminine form here, because 'shirt' is a feminine noun in Maltese. You reply:
No thanks, I want a smaller one. The shopkeeper tries again:

transcript.mt: U din? Din żgħira u ħamra.

transcript.en: And this one? Small and red. Spot both adjectives — 'small' and 'red' both in their feminine forms, matching the noun. Then: perfect, how much?
Every adjective in that scene is agreeing with its noun. That's Part 5's grammar at work in real life. 🌟
Twelve colours. Some change for gender and number, others don't — borrowed words from Italian stay the same. Two groups.
GROUP 1: colours that change — masculine / feminine / plural. White:
GROUP 2: colours that stay the same — borrowed from Italian, no change needed. Easy wins! 🎉
blue — same for masculine, feminine and plural.
Pro tip: when a colour ends in '-a' in the singular — like the three you just heard — that's a sign it never changes. See the pattern and half the work is done. 🎯
Eleven opposite pairs covering size, quality, temperature and more. Each one has a masculine, feminine and plural form — same pattern as colours. Let's hear a few.
These pairs are among the most useful words in the language. Know these ten pairs and you can describe almost anything. 💪
Nine everyday clothes items — perfect for shopping or describing someone. 🛒
Combine any of these with a colour and an adjective and you can describe any outfit. A big blue bag:
Here's the big rule of Unit 4. In Maltese, **every noun is either masculine or feminine**, and the describing word **bends to match**.
Rough guide: nouns ending in '-a' tend to be feminine — woman, car, glass are all examples. Most other endings lean masculine.
So: a masculine noun gets the masculine adjective form. A book is masculine in Maltese. White book:
A car is feminine. White car:
And walls are plural. White walls:
Three forms of one colour, three different endings. English never does this — so give yourself a few reps before expecting it to feel automatic. 🎯
And remember: the adjective comes **AFTER** the noun in Maltese. Noun first, description second. So 'a tall man':
Same adjective, three shapes. One rule: it follows the noun. 🌟
The six borrowed colour words — the ones that stay the same regardless of gender — are the easy wins of the unit.
A clothes shop dialogue. Twelve colours. Eleven opposite adjective pairs. Nine clothes items. And the adjective agreement rule. That's Unit 4.
The exercises will lock in which colours change and which don't. You've got this! 💪
